planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; beautyberry is highly adaptable and extremely drought tolerant once established — it is one of the easiest and most rewarding native shrubs for the Southeast.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; American beautyberry is one of the most spectacular and distinctive native shrubs of the Southeast, producing arching branches studded with tight clusters of brilliant magenta-purple berries along the stem from late summer through fall — a display that is genuinely unique among temperate shrubs. The berries are a critical wildlife food source eaten by over 40 bird species including mockingbirds, robins, catbirds, and quail, as well as deer, raccoons, and foxes. Leaves crushed and rubbed on skin act as a natural mosquito and deer-tick repellent (the active compounds allantoin and callicarpenal have been confirmed by USDA research). Berries can be made into jelly. Cut plants back to 12 inches in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and maximize the berry display on new wood.
sources: USDA ARS National Natural Products Research

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant to fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after frost danger passes. In zone 9–11 plants often behave as short-lived perennials and reseed freely.

directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant to fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; cape gooseberry (distinct from the ground cherry, Physalis pruinosa) is a South American native that produces papery lantern-like husks enclosing golden-yellow berries with a complex sweet-tart tropical flavor — a prized superfood fruit rich in vitamins A, C, and B-complex and antioxidant phytochemicals. The sprawling, velvety-leaved plants self-support loosely and are best staked or caged; they produce prolifically over a long season. In zone 9–11 they sometimes overwinter and behave as short-lived perennials. Berries drop naturally when ripe and can be stored in their husks for weeks. Widely used fresh, in smoothies, jams, and as a garnish. A productive warm-season berry for the Gulf Coast food garden.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 3–5 inches deep in moist, rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade after frost danger passes; in zone 8–11 rhizomes can remain in the ground year-round with light mulch.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; canna lilies are among the most spectacular and versatile tropical perennials for southern gardens, producing bold, paddle-like leaves in green, bronze, or striped and large, iris-like flowers in red, orange, yellow, pink, or bicolor from summer through frost. Dozens of named cultivars are available in heights from 18 inches ('Dwarf' types) to 8 feet, suiting them for containers, borders, water's edge, and bold accent planting. In zone 7–11 the rhizomes overwinter in the ground with mulch and re-emerge vigorously each spring. The rhizomes multiply rapidly; divide every 2–3 years. The large tropical leaves make canna an outstanding foliage plant even out of bloom.

planting.note: Root 12–18 inch stem cuttings in well-drained, sandy or cactus-mix soil with at least 4 hours direct sun; allow cut end to callus 3–5 days before planting. A sturdy 5-foot post or trellis is needed as a climbing support from the start.

directions: Root cuttings in extremely well-drained, sandy or amended cactus soil in full sun; dragon fruit is a climbing cactus native to Central America that produces spectacular night-blooming white flowers 12 inches across, followed by the familiar large, brightly colored fruit with its distinctive pink-magenta or yellow skin and speckled white or red flesh. Plants take 1–2 years from cuttings to first fruit, then produce prolifically. The vine-like stems climb via aerial roots and require a sturdy post or trellis. In zone 9a, protect from hard freezes with frost cloth — stems will die back at 28°F but established plants often resprout from the base. Fruit ripens 30–50 days after flowering; harvest when the pink color fully develops and the wings begin to wilt. Multiple varieties exist including red-fleshed 'American Beauty' and yellow-skinned Selenicereus megalanthus.

common_name: Monstera (Swiss Cheese Plant, Fruit Salad Plant)

planting.note: Root stem cuttings with at least one node in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in deep shade to partial shade; monstera grows naturally as a forest understory vine and performs best with dappled light or bright indirect light — direct sun in warm climates bleaches and burns the foliage.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in deep to bright indirect shade; monstera is one of the world's most recognizable houseplants in temperate climates but grows as a large, vigorous outdoor vine in zone 10–12, where it can climb 20 feet or more up trees, walls, and structures by aerial roots. In the wild it migrates from deep shade toward brighter light as it matures, producing the characteristic deeply lobed and perforated leaves only in brighter conditions — deeply shaded plants have smaller, unlobed juvenile leaves. The edible 'fruit' is actually a cone-like cluster of hexagonal segments that ripens from the base upward over 10–12 months; fully ripe segments slip off cleanly and taste like a blend of pineapple, banana, and coconut. Unripe fruit contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense mouth irritation — harvest only segments that have separated naturally from the base.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red mulberry grows naturally along stream banks, woodland edges, and moist bottomland and tolerates partial shade better than most fruit trees.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; the red mulberry is the only mulberry native to eastern North America and produces berries superior in flavor and nutrition to the introduced white mulberry — richer, more complex, and deeply red to black when fully ripe. It is an ecologically critical plant, providing fruit for hundreds of wildlife species at a critical early-summer period. The berries are edible fresh or made into pies, jams, wine, and dried fruit; harvest is best done by spreading a tarp and shaking the branches, as berries stain everything they touch. Red mulberry is distinguished from white mulberry by its leaves, which are rough on the upper surface (smooth in white mulberry). It unfortunately hybridizes readily with the invasive white mulberry where the two grow together, threatening the genetic integrity of pure wild populations; purchasing certified native red mulberry trees from reputable nurseries is important.

planting.note: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for Gulf Coast gardens; Mysore raspberry fruits primarily in cool weather — fall through spring — making it the ideal raspberry for zone 9–10 where temperate raspberries fail in summer heat.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Mysore raspberry is the premier fruiting bramble for subtropical gardens — the only raspberry-type fruit that thrives and produces abundantly in zone 9–10 conditions where standard raspberries fail from summer heat and disease. Native to the hills of South Asia, it produces arching, thorny canes 5–8 feet tall with attractive silver-backed leaves and large clusters of small, dark-red to black berries with an intense raspberry flavor from October through April. Unlike temperate raspberries, it fruits on second-year canes (floricanes) but in a warm-climate evergreen pattern. Cut canes to the ground after fruiting; the plant regrows vigorously. It can be invasive outside cultivation where conditions suit it — avoid planting near natural areas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; seagrape is extraordinarily salt- and drought-tolerant once established and grows in pure sand just above the tide line on Florida's coasts.

directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; seagrape is one of the most iconic plants of Florida and Caribbean coastal areas — a native evergreen shrub or small tree with enormous, nearly round leaves 6–12 inches across with distinctive red veining that deepen to a rich burgundy in winter. It grows naturally in coastal dune scrub and maritime hammocks, one of the very few trees adapted to grow in pure beach sand with salt spray exposure. The clusters of grape-like fruits ripen to deep purple in late summer and fall, eaten fresh (moderately tart) or made into jelly and wine. Seagrape is critical habitat for coastal wildlife and an important sea-turtle nesting beach plant; it is legally protected in Florida — never collect from wild populations. It is used extensively as a hedge, screen, and windbreak in zone 10–11 coastal gardens.

planting.note: Plant 12–18 inch cane sections ('seed cane') with 2–3 nodes horizontally in a furrow 3–4 inches deep; joints sprout in 2–3 weeks in warm soil. In zone 9a, plant by early May to allow maximum growth before the first frost.

directions: Plant cane cuttings ('seed cane') horizontally 3–4 inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in full sun; sugarcane is an enormous, bamboo-like tropical grass that has been cultivated for thousands of years for its sugar-rich stems. In the Gulf Coast home garden it is harvested in fall (October–November) when the stems are at peak sweetness, cut at ground level, and either pressed for fresh juice or chewed directly. The 'ratoon' growth from established root clumps often produces for several years before replanting. Most home gardeners in zone 9a treat it as an annual or protect the root clumps over winter with heavy mulch. Heirloom varieties popular in the Gulf South include 'Louisiana Purple,' 'Green German,' and 'Home Green,' all available from local growers and Southern specialty nurseries. Excellent as a tall seasonal privacy screen in the vegetable garden.

common_name: Ambarella (June Plum, Golden Apple, Otaheite Apple)

planting.note: Plant air-layered or grafted trees in spring in very well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; ambarella grows quickly and requires protection from frost — only suited for frost-free zone 10–11 gardens or large containers moved indoors in winter.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; ambarella is a fast-growing tropical fruit tree native to Melanesia and widely cultivated throughout the Pacific islands, Caribbean, and Southeast Asia for its versatile fruit. The oval, golden-yellow to orange fruits are eaten at all stages: the crisp, acidic green fruit is popular in Southeast Asian salads, slaws, and pickles with a tart mango-apple flavor, while fully ripe fruits are sweeter and eaten fresh. The fibrous pit germinates reliably in a few weeks. Ambarella is one of the faster-fruiting tropical trees, often bearing within 2–3 years from air-layers. It drops leaves briefly in dry season. In zone 9a it grows in a large container (25+ gallon) and must be moved indoors or into a greenhouse before the first frost; in zone 10+ it is an outstanding productive landscape tree.
